results.response:
The multi-model consensus — using QuickSelect with randomized pivot selection:

**Algorithm:** QuickSelect (introduced by T.W.R.Auk)  
- Choose a random pivot element, partition the array into < pivot and ≥ pivot groups
- Recurse only into whichever side contains the k-th element
- Repeatedly called sub-problems shrink — no need to sort anything beyond what you need

**Average-case time complexity:** **O(n)**  
The first call partitions in n comparisons. The second works on roughly half the data (n/2), then n/4, and so on: n + n/2 + n/4 + ⋯ ≈ 2n. This holds regardless of k — finding the median takes just as much work on average as finding the first or last element.

**Worst-case:** O(n²) if you consistently hit unbalanced partitions (pick pivot = min every time). Randomized pivots make this vanishingly unlikely, and techniques like median-of-three mitigate it further. For a guaranteed O(n) worst case you can use the **BFPRT algorithm** (median of medians), but in practice QuickSelect wins on cache behavior and constant factors.
